correctness.rationale: The two highest-leverage conversions (kJ -> kcal at /4.184 and meters -> miles at /1609.344) are correct and use the exact universal factors. WHOOP RMSSD and Garmin time-weighted HRV are read from their own columns and never blended in queries.ts. The one material correctness issue is getActivities() filtering Garmin's wall-clock-as-UTC start_time_local column with a true-UTC ISO string from new Date().toISOString() -- this is exactly the 'timestamp labeled as something else' trap CLAUDE.md and CONTEXT.md flagged. Net effect: a 30-day window silently becomes ~30d + (-utc_offset) days, so the activities page can miss or include extra recent rows depending on ET/UTC drift.

robustness.rationale: pds.unit_to_mg_factor returns NULL for IU/oz/mL/drops/scoops/capsules with no logging, no fallback, no telemetry -- a labeled Vitamin D capsule simply doesn't appear in the compound rollup. formatDuration treats 0 as missing (`if (!seconds) return '—'`) which collides with legitimate zero-minute days (e.g. sedentary_seconds=0 highly_active edge cases). formatDistance has the same `if (!meters)` falsy issue. formatPace handles zero but not negative or absurdly small speeds. The getWhoopCaloriesBurnt 'last-write-wins' on the rare two-cycle-per-day case is documented but silently discards one cycle's kJ instead of summing them.

scalability.rationale: Conversion concentrated in two layers: format.ts for display, getWhoopCaloriesBurnt for kJ->kcal at query time. No chart-layer reinvention visible here. Body weight kg/lb conversion lives in helpers (kgToLb/lbToKg referenced in CLAUDE.md but not shown in this bundle -- have to take that on faith). Spotify duration_ms -> minutes is converted at three independent call sites (getSpotifyKpis line 564, getSpotifyTopArtists line 789, getSpotifyTopTracks line 862) -- mild drift risk if the unit ever changes.

idiomaticness.rationale: Magic numbers 4.184 (kJ/kcal), 1609.344 (m/mi), 2.20462 (kg/lb, in CLAUDE.md), 60000 (ms/min), 3_600_000 (ms/hr), 18*60*60*1000 (gap window) appear as inline literals across queries.ts and format.ts with no shared constants module. The kJ/kcal factor has an inline comment, m/mi does not, ms/min is repeated. No KCAL_PER_KJ / METERS_PER_MILE / MS_PER_MIN constants. CLAUDE.md is the de-facto source-of-truth document but a code-level constants file would be one Find-Usages away from a single rename if the spec ever evolves.

summary: Onyx's unit-handling layer is in good shape on the high-leverage axes: the kJ -> kcal (4.184) and meters -> miles (1609.344) conversions are exact and used at one well-documented site each; the three semantically distinct HRV columns (whoop_recovery.hrv_rmssd_milli, garmin_hrv.last_night_avg_ms, eight_sleep_trends.avg_hrv) are read from their own tables and never composed or averaged in queries.ts. getWhoopCaloriesBurnt has an exemplary docstring naming WHOOP as canonical and citing the ET +12h cycle rule -- this is the kind of self-documenting helper that prevents future-Riley (or future-Claude) from reaching for Garmin's total_kilocalories instead. The strongest finding is structural rather than wrong-number-today: getActivities() filters Garmin's start_time_local column (wall-clock-labeled-as-UTC per CLAUDE.md) with since.toISOString() (a true-UTC instant). That's a labeled-timestamp mismatch -- the same class of bug that motivated the entire sleep_start GMT/Local fix CLAUDE.md describes. Below that, pds.unit_to_mg_factor's silent-NULL behavior for unrecognized units (IU, oz, mL, drops, scoops, capsules) is the supplements pipeline's biggest blind spot: a row with unit='IU' lands in supplement_intake fine but contributes ZERO to supplement_intake_by_compound's compound rollup with no warning, no log, no badge -- it just disappears from the daily_supplement_matrix and the causal-inference treatment enumeration. Since Vitamin D, Vitamin A, and Vitamin E are commonly IU-labeled on US bottles, this is a P1 -- a near-miss the first time Riley logs one of them from a non-DSLD seed. Other findings cluster in the brittle-but-not-wrong bucket: formatDuration / formatDistance using `if (!x)` falsy guards that swallow legitimate zero, the absence of a constants module (4.184, 1609.344, 60000, 3_600_000 are inline literals at 5+ sites), and duration_ms -> minutes conversions repeated at three Spotify call sites without a shared helper. Things done well: the WHOOP-canonical-for-TDEE rule materialized in getWhoopCaloriesBurnt with a docstring; conversion arithmetic at the query/format boundary not the chart layer; the formatPace 60-second-rollover edge case (59.5s rounds to 60 -> +1 minute, secs=0) handled explicitly.

findings.title: getActivities() filters Garmin wall-clock-as-UTC column with true-UTC instant
findings.description: getActivities() does `.gte('start_time_local', since.toISOString())` and orders by the same. Per CLAUDE.md: 'Garmin start_time_local: stored as local wall-clock labeled as UTC (+00); ::date yields ET date directly.' So start_time_local is NOT a true UTC instant -- it's a wall-clock value that Postgres serializes with a +00 offset. Comparing it to since.toISOString() (a real UTC instant ~4-5 hours behind ET) produces a silent off-by-N-hours window. A user requesting 'last 30 days' from the activities page gets a window that's actually ~30 days + offset, shifted in the wrong direction depending on the user's current UTC offset.
findings.evidence: Line 66: .gte('start_time_local', since.toISOString()). new Date() returns now in UTC, .toISOString() emits Z-suffixed UTC; start_time_local is the +00-labeled local wall-clock. The two are not comparable as instants. Mirror pattern exists correctly at line 220 (start_time_gmt against sinceISO -- both real UTC).
findings.recommendation: Either (a) switch the filter+order to start_time_gmt (true UTC) and post-process display from start_time_local, or (b) compute the since boundary as a local-wall-clock string (since.toISOString().split('T')[0] + 'T00:00:00+00') if the activities UI needs ET-day semantics. Add a regression test: a 30-day window should always return N rows where N is the count of garmin_activities with ET-date >= today - 30d.

findings.title: unit_to_mg_factor silently drops IU/oz/mL/drops/scoops/capsules from supplement rollup
findings.description: The function returns NULL for any unit not in {mg, g, mcg/ug, kg}. supplement_intake_by_compound filters NULL out (any reasonable rollup would), so a supplement logged with unit='IU' (Vitamin D, A, E -- ubiquitous on US bottles), 'oz', 'mL', 'drops', 'scoops', or 'capsules' is INVISIBLE to daily_supplement_matrix, the causal-inference treatment enumeration, supplement_*_amount binary treatments, and the compound impact charts. No warning, no log, no UI affordance.
findings.evidence: SQL_UNITS.md lines 13-30 enumerate the recognized set. CONTEXT.md explicitly flags IU. CLAUDE.md describes supplement_intake_by_compound as the rollup that 'just works' for cross-product summation via UNII -- but only for units in the dictionary. IU->mcg is compound-specific (Vit D: 1 IU = 0.025 mcg; Vit A retinol: 1 IU = 0.3 mcg; Vit E: 1 IU = 0.67 mg for alpha-tocopherol) so unit_to_mg_factor can't be a pure unit table for IU -- the conversion has to live in the (UNII, label_unit) join. But oz, mL, drops, scoops, capsules are not even attempted.
findings.recommendation: (1) Add a unit_drops audit view `pds.supplement_intake_unmapped` = SELECT product_id, ingredient_group, label_unit, COUNT(*) FROM supplement_intake JOIN supplement_products WHERE unit_to_mg_factor(label_unit) IS NULL GROUP BY 1,2,3 -- expose on /status as a drop counter. (2) Add a (unii_code, source_unit, target_unit, factor) lookup table so IU can convert per-vitamin. (3) For volumetric units (mL, drops, oz), require label density in the product seed so the conversion can be (volume * density_mg_per_ml). (4) Log NULL returns to sync_log.

findings.title: formatDuration treats 0 seconds as missing, swallowing legitimate zero
findings.description: `if (!seconds) return '—'` collapses three cases into one: null (no data), undefined (no data), AND 0 (zero seconds of activity). A genuinely sedentary day with highly_active_seconds=0 will render as '—' instead of '0m', misleadingly indistinguishable from a missing-data day. Same falsy guard at formatDistance line 66.
findings.evidence: format.ts:43 `export function formatDuration(seconds: number | null): string { if (!seconds) return '—'; ...}`. Contrast with formatShortDuration line 54 which uses the correct `seconds == null || Number.isNaN(seconds)` guard.
findings.recommendation: Replace `if (!seconds)` with `if (seconds == null || Number.isNaN(seconds))` in both formatDuration and formatDistance. Same change for formatPace line 73 if a true-zero speed should ever render differently from a null.

findings.title: Magic conversion constants scattered, no shared module
findings.description: 4.184 (kJ per kcal), 1609.344 (m per mi), 60000 (ms per minute), 3_600_000 (ms per hour), 18*60*60*1000 (workout-to-sleep gap window) appear as inline literals. No KCAL_PER_KJ, METERS_PER_MILE, MS_PER_MINUTE, MS_PER_HOUR constants. 2.20462 (kg/lb, per CLAUDE.md) lives in unseen helpers. Risk: a future contributor types 4.18 or 1609 by mistake; nothing catches it.
findings.evidence: queries.ts:360 `const kcal = Math.round(Number(c.kilojoule) / 4.184);` format.ts:67 `const miles = meters / 1609.344;` format.ts:74 `const minPerMile = 1609.344 / speedMps / 60;` queries.ts:564 `totalHours: totalMs / 3_600_000` queries.ts:789,862 `entry.minutes += (r.duration_ms ?? 0) / 60000;`
findings.recommendation: Create frontend/src/lib/units.ts exporting `export const KCAL_PER_KJ = 1 / 4.184; export const METERS_PER_MILE = 1609.344; export const MS_PER_MINUTE = 60_000; export const MS_PER_HOUR = 3_600_000; export const POUNDS_PER_KG = 2.20462;` Each with a single-line citation comment (e.g. // CODATA 2018; NIST handbook). Import everywhere these literals currently appear.

findings.title: getWhoopCaloriesBurnt rounds at query time, losing sub-kcal precision
findings.description: `const kcal = Math.round(Number(c.kilojoule) / 4.184);` rounds to integer kcal at the query layer. Per CLAUDE.md/CONTEXT.md, the convention is conversion-once-at-display. If any downstream consumer wants to sum 7 days for a weekly TDEE or compute a rolling 30d avg, the rounding propagates and accumulates a ~0.5 kcal per day bias.
findings.evidence: queries.ts:360. The accompanying object keeps both `kilojoule` (raw) and `calories_burnt` (rounded). Consumers that sum calories_burnt accumulate up to N*0.5 kcal of rounding error over N days; consumers that recompute from kilojoule are fine but bypass the helper's intent.
findings.recommendation: Keep `calories_burnt` as the full float (no Math.round) and round only at display via a formatKcal helper. Or move kcal computation entirely into a view (`pds.whoop_cycles_v` with a generated kcal column = kilojoule / 4.184) so no client ever has to remember.

findings.title: getWhoopCaloriesBurnt last-write-wins drops second cycle on rare two-cycle days
findings.description: The comment acknowledges 'last-write-wins picks the later cycle on the rare day with two; in practice there's one cycle per ET date.' But 'rare' isn't 'never' -- the WHOOP-cycle TZ backfill machinery in CLAUDE.md exists precisely because cycles span time zones and a transition day can carry an arrival nap + main cycle. Silently overwriting one cycle's kilojoule with another's underreports TDEE on transition days specifically -- the days where the user's behavioral state is most unusual and therefore most analytically interesting.
findings.evidence: queries.ts:362-363. Compare with the `pds.daily_health_matrix_behavioral` view's LATERAL pattern (CLAUDE.md describes 'WHOOP cycle deduplicated via LATERAL ... LIMIT 1 longest cycle per behavioral day') -- the SQL side handles transition days explicitly; the kcal helper does not.
findings.recommendation: Sum kilojoule per behavioral_date instead of replace: `const existing = byDate.get(calendar_date); byDate.set(calendar_date, { calendar_date, kilojoule: (existing?.kilojoule ?? 0) + Number(c.kilojoule), calories_burnt: ... });` Then compute kcal from the summed kilojoule at the end. Match the behavioral-day aggregation rule rather than relying on the rarity argument.

findings.title: Spotify duration_ms -> minutes conversion repeated at 3 sites with inline /60000
findings.description: Three different aggregation helpers (getSpotifyKpis, getSpotifyTopArtists, getSpotifyTopTracks) each do `(r.duration_ms ?? 0) / 60000` or `/ 3_600_000` inline. If duration_ms ever switches column name (e.g. duration_sec, duration_us), or the storage convention changes, the rename is a 3-site update with no compiler nudge.
findings.evidence: queries.ts:564 `const totalMs = rows.reduce((s, r) => s + (r.duration_ms ?? 0), 0);` then `totalHours: totalMs / 3_600_000`. queries.ts:789 `entry.minutes += (r.duration_ms ?? 0) / 60000;` queries.ts:862 same.
findings.recommendation: `export const msToMinutes = (ms: number | null) => (ms ?? 0) / 60_000;` in the units module. Use everywhere.

findings.title: etDate() called on a synthetic ISO string from getTime()+shift introduces silent UTC round-trip
findings.description: whoopSleepDay does `new Date(startTime).getTime() - 6 * 3600 * 1000` then `.toISOString()` then etDate(). The intermediate ISO conversion is needed only to satisfy etDate's signature; the Date.toISOString round-trip is fine semantically but wastes work and obscures the actual computation. More importantly, etDate's docstring says callers should pass `sleep.end_time` (wake-day attribution), but whoopSleepDay subtracts 6h from start_time -- which is a different rule. Both rules can be correct for their own use cases but the comment-vs-code coupling is fragile.
findings.evidence: format.ts:11-19 (etDate comment: 'callers should pass sleep.end_time'). format.ts:37-41 (whoopSleepDay passes start_time minus 6h, which is the ADR-0001 behavioral-day rule, NOT the wake-day rule the etDate doc describes).
findings.recommendation: Either (a) rename etDate's docstring to drop the end_time-vs-start_time guidance and just describe it as `instant -> ET calendar date`, or (b) add a behavioralDate() helper alongside etDate() with its own docstring explicitly citing ADR-0001's -6h rule. Avoid two different date-attribution rules sharing one function.

findings.title: formatDate constructs Date from 'YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00' (no TZ) -> renders in browser-local TZ
findings.description: `new Date(dateStr + 'T00:00:00')` parses as local time per JS spec, so the date renders correctly for any user in ET but would render off-by-one for a user east of UTC (because midnight local of dateStr there becomes the previous UTC day, but the toLocaleDateString call reformats in the same local TZ so it usually round-trips). The bug surface is small for an n=1 American single-user, but the function is innocuous-looking and gets copy-pasted.
findings.evidence: format.ts:1-4. Compare with shiftDate (lines 24-30) which correctly uses Date.UTC to sidestep TZ.
findings.recommendation: Use the same UTC pattern: `const [y, m, d] = dateStr.split('-').map(Number); return new Date(Date.UTC(y, m-1, d)).toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'short', day: 'numeric', timeZone: 'UTC' });` Explicit, no implicit local-TZ branch.

findings.title: getWorkoutSleepGap pred_date computed from cycleStart -1 day with no TZ-aware shift
findings.description: `const predDateMs = cycleStartMs - 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000; const predDate = new Date(predDateMs).toLocaleDateString('en-CA', { timeZone: 'America/New_York' });` This works for ~95% of days but on a DST transition the 24-hour subtraction can land on the wrong day (the ET day boundary shifted by 1h during spring/fall transition). Same TZ-arithmetic landmine that ADR-0001 was written to eliminate.
findings.evidence: queries.ts:262-268. The cycle_id is right there; the loader could have read cycle_date from whoop_cycles directly instead of recomputing from start_time, which would inherit whatever ET-correct logic the trigger already applied.
findings.recommendation: Read whoop_cycles.cycle_date (the trigger-populated ET cycle date) instead of recomputing from start_time. If that column isn't selected, add it: `.select('cycle_id,start_time,cycle_date')`. Then `predDate = cycle_date` (or `shiftDate(cycle_date, -1)` if -1 is intended).

findings.title: Three HRV columns correctly isolated -- but no shared type guard prevents future conflation
findings.description: queries.ts reads whoop_recovery.hrv_rmssd_milli (line 216) and exposes it as next_morning_hrv (line 275). getHrvData (line 45) reads garmin_hrv. getEightSleepTrends (line 299) reads eight_sleep_trends.avg_hrv. Three sources, three columns, three names -- correctly kept separate. But there's no TypeScript brand/nominal type stopping someone from passing a WHOOP RMSSD value into a function expecting Garmin's time-weighted HRV. The next_morning_hrv field on WorkoutSleepGap is typed `number | null` -- semantically RMSSD but the type can't enforce it.
findings.evidence: queries.ts:194-201 (WorkoutSleepGap interface), line 275 (assignment from cycleHrv). The type signature next_morning_hrv: number gives no hint about which HRV definition. CLAUDE.md explicitly says 'never average or substitute' the three.
findings.recommendation: Use TypeScript branded types: `type WhoopRmssdMs = number & { __brand: 'WhoopRmssdMs' }; type GarminHrvMs = number & { __brand: 'GarminHrvMs' };` Then `next_morning_hrv: WhoopRmssdMs | null` etc. The compiler will refuse to assign a GarminHrvMs to a WhoopRmssdMs slot. Cheap, no runtime cost, eliminates the entire conflation class.

findings.title: formatPace lacks sanity guard for absurdly slow speeds (returns 60+ minutes)
findings.description: formatPace handles 0 and null but not a tiny positive number like 0.01 m/s (which would give 2682 min/mi). A jammed GPS or a paused-but-not-stopped activity can produce these. The display would render '2682:00 /mi' -- not wrong per se, but unhelpful.
findings.evidence: format.ts:72-79. Compare with formatShortDuration which caps at h:mm:ss but doesn't bound the h.
findings.recommendation: Add `if (minPerMile > 30) return '—';` or render as 'walking' / 'paused' if pace exceeds a configurable threshold. Add the same lower-bound check (no faster than world-record pace ~3:43/mi).

things_done_well: getWhoopCaloriesBurnt docstring explicitly names WHOOP as canonical and Garmin's total_kilocalories as the wrong-answer alternative, citing the ET +12h cycle rule. This is the kind of self-documenting helper that prevents future regressions and is exactly what a unit-conversion audit hopes to find.
kJ -> kcal factor 4.184 is exact (matches NIST). Meters -> miles 1609.344 is exact (international mile). No rounded approximations.
format.ts:formatPace handles the 60-second rollover (59.5s rounds to 60, increment minute, zero seconds) -- the rare edge case that would otherwise produce '7:60 /mi'.

Three semantically distinct HRV columns (whoop_recovery.hrv_rmssd_milli, garmin_hrv.last_night_avg_ms, eight_sleep_trends.avg_hrv) are read from their own tables; no fill-missing/coalesce/average logic mixes them in queries.ts. CLAUDE.md's 'never average or substitute' rule is honored in this file.
Distance conversion gracefully degrades to meters under 0.5mi rather than rendering '0.3 mi' (format.ts:68-69). Good UX choice.
shiftDate explicitly uses Date.UTC to dodge TZ/DST edges with a clear comment explaining why (format.ts:21-23). This is the right pattern for date-string arithmetic.
getSpotifyKpis derives totalHours from totalMs / 3_600_000 -- a single conversion, not double-converted via minutes. Avoids the rounding-chain trap.
getEightSleepTrends correctly reads the typed avg_hrv field and exposes it as-is; CLAUDE.md notes Eight Sleep's HRV is undocumented and this code doesn't pretend to know what it is.
formatShortDuration uses `seconds == null || Number.isNaN(seconds)` -- the correct guard (vs. formatDuration's incorrect `!seconds` falsy guard). Some of the file already knows the right pattern.
questions_for_followup: Does any chart on /analytics/hrv or /sleep coalesce/fill garmin_hrv into whoop_hrv (or vice versa) when one source is missing? Bundle shows queries are isolated but page-level rendering wasn't shown.

What is the actual count of pds.supplement_intake rows where unit_to_mg_factor(label_unit) IS NULL? Even a heuristic floor (e.g. 'every Vitamin D row Riley has logged') would calibrate F-002's severity.

kgToLb / lbToKg helpers referenced in CLAUDE.md but not present in this bundle. Are they 2.20462 exactly, or 2.2? And do they round before final display (which would be a precision-loss bug invisible to this audit)?

Is there any place that reads garmin_sleep.sleep_start_local (the deprecated wall-clock-as-UTC field) anywhere on the frontend? The bundle showed getSleepData selects '*' from garmin_sleep -- if downstream components access the local column they'd inherit the TZ shift CLAUDE.md flagged.
Does pds.supplement_intake_by_compound surface a 'unrecognized_unit' diagnostic, or is the silent NULL drop truly silent (no metric, no alarm, no /status panel)?
duration_ms vs duration_seconds vs duration_milli: how many distinct duration unit columns exist across the schema, and is there a lint rule preventing a new helper from reading the wrong one?

Does the activities page (/activities) actually exhibit the off-by-N-hours window symptom from F-001? A quick check: a query for last 1 day at 11 PM ET should return today's activities; if it sometimes returns yesterday's, F-001 is observably wrong.
